title: Stale Compound exchange rate lets new deposits mint inflated shares and steal accrued yield
claim: Pool accounting prices cUSDT with `exchangeRateStored()` instead of an interest-accruing current rate. When Compound interest has accrued but the stored rate is stale, `deposit()` computes `pool` too low and mints too many shares to the new depositor. A later Compound interaction refreshes the exchange rate, letting those inflated shares redeem previously accrued yield from existing holders.
impact: Previously accrued Compound yield can be transferred from incumbent shareholders to a new depositor, causing direct dilution and theft of vault value.
paths: Vault assets sit in Compound long enough for `exchangeRateStored()` to lag the real exchange rate.
Attacker calls `deposit(_amount)` while `pool` is understated and receives excess shares.
Attacker or any later interaction touches Compound and refreshes the exchange rate.
Those excess shares can then be withdrawn for more USDT than the attacker contributed.

title: Any accounted asset donation can permanently brick deposits when total supply is zero
claim: `deposit()` decides whether to bootstrap on `pool == 0` instead of `_totalSupply == 0`. If the vault holds any asset counted by `_calcPoolValueInToken()` while `_totalSupply` is zero, the function takes the non-bootstrap path and computes `shares = _amount * 0 / pool = 0`, so the depositor receives no shares.
impact: A dust donation before the first deposit, or after a full exit, can permanently brick the vault: later depositors transfer in funds, receive zero shares, and cannot recover the locked assets.
paths: Attacker transfers USDT or another accounted vault asset to the contract while `_totalSupply == 0`.
`pool` is now non-zero, so the contract skips the bootstrap branch and mints zero shares.
No shares exist to redeem the accumulated assets, leaving the vault stuck.

title: Public strategy entrypoints can strand idle funds in a non-provider lender and DOS withdrawals
claim: `supplyDydx`, `supplyAave`, `supplyFulcrum`, and `supplyCompound` are public and can invest whatever idle USDT is sitting in the vault, but they do not update `provider`. Pool valuation still counts those side positions, while `withdraw()` can only source liquidity through `_withdrawSome()` from the lender stored in `provider` (or from no lender at all when `provider == NONE`).
impact: An attacker can permissionlessly strand newly deposited idle funds in a lender that `withdraw()` does not use, causing partial or full withdrawal reverts and potentially leaving a slice of vault assets inaccessible until `provider` changes and `_withdrawAll()` happens.
paths: Users deposit and the vault keeps fresh USDT idle because deposits do not auto-invest.
Attacker calls a public `supply*` function for a lender different from `provider`, or while `provider == NONE`.
`_calcPoolValueInToken()` still includes those assets, so withdrawals are priced as if they were available.
`_withdrawSome()` only withdraws from `provider`, so once on-hand plus provider liquidity is insufficient, `withdraw()` reverts while side-position funds remain stranded.

title: dYdX balances are treated as assets even if the account turns negative
claim: Both `balanceDydx()` and `_balanceDydx()` ignore the `Wei.sign` field returned by dYdX and always add `bal.value` into pool accounting as though it were a positive asset.
impact: If the configured dYdX account were ever negative, share pricing and solvency checks would overstate assets, which could lead to impossible withdrawal amounts and reverts.
paths: The dYdX account for `dToken` reports `sign == false`.
The vault still adds `bal.value` to total pool value.
Deposits and withdrawals are then priced against overstated assets, and later redemptions may fail.
